_comment: Auto-generated in-character comments on Field Report articles. Keyed by article slug. Each comment is by another staff member (not the article author), in their voice, max 220 chars, capped 3 per article.

the-case-for-functional-girth.text: Structurally sound. "Decorative girth" vs "functional girth" is a distinction the catalogue, properly understood, has needed since volume one. I remain skeptical of the specific entries, pending review.
Bertram made this exact point to Philippe Marchand at the '94 Geneva salon, standing next to a Bugatti EB110. Nobody listened. Eli, darling, you've essentially reinvented him. I'm not upset.

physics-of-thiccc.text: Teddy citing physics to explain why big things are shaped like big things is very on-brand. The square-cube law has been operating without his help for some time. That said: correct.

bagger-288-thicccest-machine.text: The Bagger 288 is incredible, yes. It is also one machine. A bollard is thiccc every single day, in every weather, without requiring its own zip code.

foods-of-substance.text: The word "organized" is doing considerable work in that dek. Considerable work.

anatomy-thiccc-vehicle.text: Five parts. Bold of a vehicle to have exactly five parts. This writer counts at least nine on a cement mixer alone, and that's before the drum starts spinning.

concrete-material-study.text: Concrete appears in 23 approved entries. I have counted. This does not constitute a "material study." It constitutes an index, which is my job. I will be submitting a formal note on jurisdiction.
Recorded the word "concrete" 47 times last Tuesday. It did not change. The word remained concrete.

everyday-sidewalk-thiccc.text: Field survey methodology: unspecified. Sample size: five. Selection criteria: "sturdier than it looks," which is not a classification standard. I have filed accordingly.
Narrated this one twice. First take was clean. Second take I read "sturdier than it looks" with slightly more conviction than the script called for. I kept the second take.

taxonomy-thiccc-architecture.text: The shape doesn't, sure. But "massive" and "thiccc" are doing very different work here, and conflating them is how you end up cataloguing an airport.
Bartholomew, I nearly called you about this. My uncle Bertram owned a very thiccc building in Carmel and we never once called it massive. These are different feelings. Eli understands.

industrial-scale-thiccc.text:
HAMBACH, GERMANY

Kid, I've watched bucket-wheel excavators take bites out of a hillside at 3am. Thiccc isn't a requirement past a certain scale. It's the only language the scale speaks.

cement-trucks-original-thiccc.text: Correct on all counts. I want it noted that I said this first, in writing, in 2019, in a document the Junior Cataloguer has apparently misplaced.

field-guide-spotting-thiccc.text: Three tests. No instruments. The catalogue, properly understood, requires fourteen criteria, seven of which demand a calibrated reference object. I have filed accordingly.

the-five-thicccest-things.text: No cement mixer in the top five. This writer is filing nothing officially. Just noting that "so far" is doing a lot of work in that headline.
Reporting from a grain elevator in Duluth where the air itself is thick. Bart's list is wrong in ways I cannot prove yet. Number three should be a ship. It is always a ship.

what-counts-as-thiccc.text: Useful guide. One note: "where the line is" implies the line is settled, which it is not, which is why some of us are still arguing about cement mixers in the break room. Informally. During lunch.
Wonderful piece. Reminds me of the charter Bertram drafted in 1987, or possibly 1991. He had a similar section on "the line." He crossed it out. I've always felt that was the right call.

history-of-thiccc.text: The word predates the dictionary. The dictionary predates this publication. This publication predates any coherent editorial policy. Historiography is easy when you leave out the hard part.
